revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:岩村田祇園祭お船様祭り]]
{{Ref improve|date=October 10, 2015}} '''Iwamurada Gion Festival''' (岩村田祇園祭, Iwamurada Gion Matsuri) is a {{ill|summer festival|ja|夏祭り|1=Summer Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/summer festival|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Matsuri|qq=}} held in {{ill|Iwamurada|ja|岩村田|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamurada|1=Iwamurada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q22127357}}, {{ill|Saku, Nagano|en|Saku, Nagano|qid=Q495821|lt=Saku City|1=Saku, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}. It is centered around the Arayado District and is a type of {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}}. A sub-festival held as part of the event, the '''Ofuna-sama Festival''' (お船様祭り), has been designated an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24895900}} by Saku City.
== History == According to legend, the "Princess Fune" (船姫) of {{ill|Ōi Castle|ja|大井城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi Castle|qq=}} was self-conscious about her lion-like face and jumped to her death into {{ill|Yugawa River|ja|湯川 (北佐久郡)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugawa River|qq=}} in July. Before her death, she prayed for the eradication of an epidemic in her domain, offering her life in exchange. Her prayer was answered, and she transformed into a {{ill|Egret|en|Egret|qid=Q3060012|lt=white heron|1=Egret}}, which caused the epidemic to cease.
Several local landmarks are associated with her legend, including: * '''Shishi Rock''' (獅子岩): The cliff where she jumped. * '''Ofune-ga-Fuchi''' (御船ケ淵): The pool below the cliff. * '''Tsunegi''' (常木): Where her clothing washed ashore. * '''Tateishi''' (立石): The stone believed to be her transformed body. * '''Ofune Stone''' (御船石): A boulder from her family’s home in Karuizawa that moved to Yugawa River and is said to emit cries on her death anniversary.
In {{ill|Ōei 5|ja|応永5年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579292|1=1398}} (1398), the {{ill|Ōi clan|ja|大井氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōi clan|qq=}} introduced the Gion Festival to Iwamurada from {{ill|Tsushima Shrine|en|Tsushima Shrine|qid=Q705136|lt=Tsushima Shrine|1=Tsushima Shrine}} in {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, merging it with the Ofuna Festival. The Shishi Rock and Ofune-ga-Fuchi remain owned by the Iwamurada district head family, who host related rituals and provide offerings, including {{ill|sake|ja|清酒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sake|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本酒|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本酒}}, tofu, eel, and sweet tea, during the festival.
== Rituals and Practices == On the princess’s death anniversary, the Ofuna-sama is enshrined with its heron decoration facing the northeast direction (丑寅). A mikoshi from the Wakamiya Shrine is placed nearby, and passing beneath it is believed to grant protection from epidemics.
The festival historically involved only one mikoshi from the Arayado District, but later expanded to include mikoshi from other regions and groups.
== Timeline == * '''Ōei 5 (1398)''': Introduction of the Gion Festival from Owari Province. * '''Eiroku era (1558–1570)''': Festival interrupted by wartime unrest. * '''Kan'ei era (1624–1644)''': Festival revived after 70 years. * '''Kyōhō 2 (1716)''': Fights during the festival become problematic. * '''Tenpō 5 (1834)''': Amid a plague outbreak, a new mikoshi is commissioned in Edo and brought to Saku. * '''Meiji era (1868)''': Worship of Gozu Tennō replaced with Susanoo-no-Mikoto. * '''Shōwa 16 (1941)''': Due to the war, herons are pulled by cattle.
== Ofuna-sama Construction and Dismantling == Each July, preparations for the Iwamurada Gion Festival include constructing the Ofuna-sama, a large ship-like structure, in the middle of the road. Its design features a white heron figure atop a 7.6-meter mast. The construction materials were historically donated by the Tsuchiya family of Karuizawa but are now sourced locally. The structure is dismantled the morning after the festival.
== Designations == * In {{ill|1983|en|1983|qid=Q2434|lt=Showa 58|1=1983}}, Saku City designated the "Ofuna-sama Festival in the Iwamurada Gion Festival" as an Intangible Folk Cultural Property. * In {{ill|2012|en|2012|qid=Q1990|lt=Heisei 24|1=2012}}, the "Mikoshi of Wakamiya Shrine’s Gion Shrine" was designated a Tangible Cultural Property.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} * {{ill|Ofuna Festival|ja|お船祭り|1=Ofune Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofuna Festival|13=User:Immanuelle/Ofune Matsuri|qq=}} * {{ill|Suicide by Drowning|ja|入水|zh|自溺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suicide by Drowning|1=suicide by drowning|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11388929}}
== External Links == * [http://www.sakukankou.jp/event/iwamurada-gion/ Saku City Tourism Association - Iwamurada Gion Festival] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20160310135303/https://www.city.saku.nagano.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazai/bunkazaijimusyo/shiteibunkazai.html List of Designated Cultural Properties - Saku City]
